author: @rogergreen9784
text:
Nice video and spot on.
For me it was in initially chemisry but later switched to electronics where I took everything electronic apart in my house just to see how it works, much to the frustration of my parents. I am still learning and have just applied to do a phD and am in my fifties!!!
However I have not seen this obsession in any of the next generation of kids - I have 4 and it is hard work just to get them interested in anything, anything at all....
I am not sure whether it is nature of nurture - I have that curiousity gene but if it has been passed on it is being repressed or society has deemed it uncool/irrelevent?!?!? I do not have an answer on how we can address this, believe me, I have tried with my own kids.
I worry for he future where we no longer need to be knowleable to survive - people can quite easily go through life with basic living skills, but when things start running out or there is a global disaster then we're in trouble - it reminds me of the 'telephone sanitation engineers' from hitch hikers...
Love you comments on smoking - good on you, we are all responsible for our own lives and the way we decide to live them.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: awesome mate - i know what you mean re hitch hiker's  - by the way i think we are at our best when we are at our worst and at our worst when thigs are the best - if we do hit a calamity I am pretty sure we will shine - but i have some pretty Rousseauesque notions lol

author: @JSabh
text: I'm pretty sure you will appreciate the humor I find in starting a new interest or avenue to get me there. for instance, I recently was following a rabbit hole that lead me to planetary magnetic fields... oh boy. I am laughing thinking about the level of work involved in really studying such a vast subject because I know that I'd stop at the end of the day and wonder what just happened lol. Sure, I'll retain the information but my Lord, will I be tired. Had no idea that planetary magnetic fields go hand and hand with quantum physics. When I did learn this, I sat back and laughed as I closed that subject for now. I love learning but you know how draining it is to emmerse yourself into something as fantastically involved as quantum physics. Good to see you my friend and best wishes from the USA!

Robert if ever I get a chance I would love to have a beer with you and talk about battery chemistry.

With regards to smoking, I smoke because I enjoy it and I dont want to spend the last 30 years of my life, sitting in a chair, watching TV while wearing a diaper.

The hypocrisy of the non-smokers is astounding, 

OH NOOO he is burning a few leaves AAAHHHHH   AAAAHHHHH HE IS A TERRIBLE MAN. 

Then they go jump into their 4x4 and race off blowing billions of liters of carcinogenic gasses into the atmosphere for every one to inhale. What a bunch of dick heads.

You smoke, but at least you trying to solve the Worlds Energy Crisis, they do nothing but eat, sleep and poop.

You are  great MAN Robert, keep up the good work.

replies.author: @azurealism
replies.text:
I badgered my father to quit smoking. He smoked daily every day of his life since deploying to Vietnam in 1968. 42 years of chain smoking, and we badgered him for every cancer stick he lit up - a pack a day. 

He died in 2013 from melanoma that had metastasized to his brain. We never once badgered him about putting on sunblock. 

Light 'em up, smoke 'em if you got 'em....

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: my wife's grandmother smoked until she was 94 - yes 94 - when she got so frail she went into a home for the elderly and they refused to allow her to smoke - she died a year later

author: @wfrancis25
text:
I really think i would really like chemistry, especially now since i've been watching your channel and when i started playing with HHO gas creation (I'm an electronic radar tech). But what i could never get was the formulas of take A and B and mix them you get X and Y and Z.....Balancing the equation was easy, it's math........but I never understood how the teacher knew that you get X, Y, Z when mixing them. What would the equation be if i burned it, or whatever. I just memorized it as a parrot for exams.
My question is, Is there a way to determine or calculate what happens to A and B when mixing, burning, electrolizing, etc or is it, that you should just be good and memorize it?Thanx again for another awesome video Rob, cheers from South Africa

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @BluesDoctor
text: Very well put. Curiosity with learned discipline in the scientific method are key I believe. Some take to it and others don't. I was in intensive care some years ago for a massive lung infection. They probed my lungs with a camera and said other than local infection my lungs were pink and healthy. I have smoked since age 14. Genes, mucus production and who knows what else, but at age 63 I escaped serious harm. Not everyone is the same. I don't advocate smoking, it is a nasty habit. People should leave their snap judgements to themselves despite good intentions. Facts are facts, outliers always show up in groups of data.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - nice post

author: @VinoVeritas_
text:
I wouldn't have considered myself massively interested in chemistry 3 years ago, but I had done some home brewing, enjoyed the science of cooking and had a big interest in advancements in technology regardless of the scientific disciplines that spawned them.

That all changed when I was in Thailand surrounded by all the lush vegetation and decided to search online for some information regarding solar cells made from chlorophyll. Serendipity introduced me to your YouTube channel and I've been hooked since. I'm currently working on a Deep Eutectic Solvent and Carboxymethyl Cellulose​ gel electrolyte for Aluminium Air cells and a wholly coconut derived supercapicitor using activated carbon (coconut shell) for the electrodes and the flesh as a superdialectric.

I recently got chatting to a PhD candidate in Chemistry at Imperial College London about hydrothermal carbonisation, graphitic carbon nitride and DESs. At some point in the conversation, he asked me which university I had studied chemistry at. He was shocked when I told him that I didn't even have a chemistry GCSE.

So a big thanks to Rob, Steve, Ashley and all the new team members for sharing their knowledge and passion.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: awesome story mate lol - thanks for sharing

author: @alaingrignon4426
text:
The biggest curiosity killer is money. Take the need for money to live and there no longer a barrier for curiosity to flourish. Most people do what they do because of money. Most people are unhappy with what they do because they've trumped money over curiosity. I'm not saying, stop making money in order to regain your curiosity, just that money is the root of our decision making from a very early age.

Robert, I'm sure you didn't get into all of this because you were looking to make money, you did it because you were curious. You actually had to think extra hard on how to make money from your passion. Most people look at it the other way, myself included, how can I get money to feed my passion. That's the difference between and most of us.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you are right there mate - it is because i am interested and making money from it is extremely hard lol

replies.author: @Barskor1

author: @MartinPHellwig
text: I don't think every child is born with a natural drive to explore, they may be curious but only to a certain level. I would guesstimate that over 80% of the children have no innate desire to explore more than that what is right in front of them. The other 20% will mostly be broken willed by being thought interesting subjects by teachers who do not understand the subject themselves well enough. That only leaves a very small amount of people that had the good fortune of having either a good teacher or exceptionally stubborn. And of these only the stubborn will have any long term chance to continue. My son is now 8 years old and has lost all the passion he had for maths, geography and biology because school insist teaching the subject in a matter that is only suitable for verbal minds and not analytical minds like his, and this happened in just under a year. Even though he is in private education I think that school is doing him more harm than good.

replies.author: @MartinPHellwig
replies.text: Well I am always open to have my opinion altered, there is not a single opinion I have that I have not have revised at least two times.
